title: The Climate Immunity Doctrine: How New Zealand's Government Created a Get-Out-of-Jail-Free Card for Big Emitters
subtitle: The stated purpose is business certainty. The actual purpose is liability protection for Fonterra and six other major polluters.
author: The Siphoned Truth
content:
Adversarial Analysis | May 17, 2026

The New Zealand government's announcement on May 12, 2026 that it would amend the Climate Change Response Act 2002 to block private climate lawsuits isn't about creating "certainty." It's about creating a legal firewall for the country's largest polluters — specifically Fonterra and six other major dairy and fossil fuel companies — before a High Court case can test whether those companies owed a duty of care to New Zealanders harmed by their emissions.

The timing is the story.

The Smith Case: A Threat Too Serious to Let Reach Trial

In 2024, iwi leader Mike Smith secured Supreme Court permission to sue Fonterra and six other major emitters. His claim: that these companies' emissions — estimated at roughly one-third of New Zealand's total greenhouse gas output — had damaged his land, interests, and cultural rights. The case was sent back to the High Court. A hearing was scheduled to begin in April 2027.

Then the government acted. Announced May 12, 2026, the amendment to the Climate Change Response Act would apply to both current and future proceedings. Not future cases. Current cases. The Smith case is the obvious target.

Justice Minister Paul Goldsmith's justification: "The courts are not the right place to resolve claims of harm from climate change." The real message: the courts are not the right place to hold us accountable either.

"Business Confidence": The Magic Spell That Makes Anything Go

Note the framing Goldsmith used: litigation is "creating uncertainty in business confidence and investment." This is not an argument about legal principles. It's a pressure campaign dressed in bureaucratic language.

When the government says it wants a "clear and predictable regime," understand what that regime actually is: one where parliament sets emissions targets through the Emissions Trading Scheme, and courts are forbidden from holding anyone accountable for meeting — or more likely, failing to meet — those targets.

The EDS's Gary Taylor called it "an attack on the rule of law." He's right. More pointedly, Taylor said Goldsmith "should be ashamed of himself for bringing a bill of this kind to Parliament." And he urged the Attorney General to investigate whether the amendment is consistent with the Bill of Rights Act.

These are not fringe opinions. They are the logical conclusions of anyone who believes: - Citizens should have the right to seek remedies for harm done to them - Laws should not be retroactively changed to benefit defendants already in court - The separation of powers exists to prevent exactly this kind of executive interference with pending judicial proceedings

The ICJ Contradiction

In July 2025, the International Court of Justice issued an advisory opinion affirming that states have a legal obligation to address climate harm — and that people must be able to test those obligations in court. ClientEarth noted this in responding to New Zealand's announcement: "Restricting access to courts is bad for justice, bad for the environment, and bad for democracy and the rule of law."

New Zealand's government just told the ICJ it agrees in principle while actively working to undermine it in practice. The ICJ said access to courts is part of the climate obligation. New Zealand is removing that access.

This is the global context the government wants you to ignore. Climate litigation is surging worldwide — in Europe, the United States, Australia — as courts become the forum where citizens force accountability that legislators won't deliver. New Zealand just opted out of that global trend. Not because the courts got it wrong. Because they might get it right.

The Dairy Problem

Fonterra is New Zealand's largest company and the world's largest dairy exporter. It is also, by most estimates, one of the country's largest greenhouse gas emitters. The suggestion that such a company — responsible for one-third of national emissions — cannot be sued in New Zealand courts for the climate damage its emissions contribute to is not a legal technicality. It's a policy position: that scale alone grants immunity.

The government is essentially arguing that emissions are too politically and economically important to be subject to the normal operation of tort law. The ETS is the appropriate forum. Courts are not.

But the ETS does not compensate individual New Zealanders whose land is degraded, whose livelihoods are disrupted, whose cultural sites are threatened by climate change driven by these emissions. The ETS is a carbon pricing mechanism, not a remediation system for climate harm to specific people.

The Smith case was an attempt to create that remediation pathway through common law — to ask whether a company that emits at scale owes a duty of care to those who suffer the consequences. The government just decided that question cannot be asked in court.

Retroactive Lawmaking and the Constitution

The amendment's application to current proceedings is its most constitutionally troubling feature. The Smith case has been working through the courts since 2024. The Supreme Court ruled it should proceed. Discovery has occurred. Arguments have been prepared.

Now the government wants to change the law mid-game so the defendants can have the case dismissed on a technicality. Goldsmith says this is about preventing "two regimes" — implying the courts would create a duplicative enforcement track alongside the ETS.

But the ETS is a regulatory regime administered by the government. The Smith case is a common law duty-of-care claim by a private citizen. They are not the same thing. Goldsmith's "two regimes" argument is either a deliberate conflation or a revealing confession: that the government does not believe companies should face any enforcement mechanism for emissions-related harm beyond carbon pricing.

Which is a defensible policy position, if you believe carbon pricing adequately compensates for all climate damage. But that's not for Goldsmith to decide unilaterally. That's a question courts should be allowed to answer.

What This Actually Is

Strip away the "business confidence" rhetoric and the "clearing up uncertainty" language, and here is what happened: The New Zealand government identified its largest companies — led by Fonterra — as facing potential legal liability for climate harm. It then used its parliamentary majority to change the law to eliminate that liability before a court could rule on it.

If this sounds familiar, it should. The United States has seen a wave of "climate immunity" laws at the state level — Texas, Louisiana, Florida — specifically designed to shield fossil fuel companies from tort liability. New Zealand is now adopting the same playbook. The difference is that New Zealand is doing it to protect a dairy conglomerate rather than an oil major.

The EDS is right to call this an attack on the rule of law. It is also, more precisely, a transfer of risk from capital to citizens — a decision that the costs of climate change will be borne by people like Mike Smith, not by the companies that generated the emissions.

That is a choice a government is free to make. But it should be honest about what it is: not legal housekeeping, but a fundamental restructuring of who bears the cost of climate harm in New Zealand.

— Adversarial Analysis | t_c0a3b876
